charts.title: Week 4 Topic Map
charts.description: High-level overview of all major topics in Week 4.

nodes.label:
Week 4
Cooperative Game Theory
nodes.note: Week 4 shifts from non-cooperative to cooperative game theory. Players can form binding agreements and split jointly generated value. Key questions are which coalitions form, what value they generate, and how payoffs should be divided fairly and stably.

nodes.label:
Cooperative vs
Non-Cooperative
nodes.note: Cooperative game theory assumes binding agreements are enforceable, eliminating the defection problem. The focus moves from individual strategies to coalition formation and payoff division.

nodes.label:
TU vs NTU
Games
nodes.note: Transferable utility (TU) games allow free redistribution of value via side payments. Non-transferable utility (NTU) games fix payoffs to individuals based on coalition actions. Most classical cooperative theory assumes TU.

nodes.label:
TU Games:
Formal Definition
nodes.note: A TU game is a pair (N, v) where N is the player set and v is the characteristic function mapping every coalition to its maximum achievable value. An outcome is a coalition structure and payoff vector.

nodes.label: Game Examples
nodes.note: Canonical TU games include weighted voting games (win/lose coalitions), network flow games (max flow through coalition's edges), and graph-induced games (internal edge weights).

nodes.label:
Special Game
Classes
nodes.note: Important classes: simple games (0-1 values), superadditive games (merging never hurts, incentivising grand coalition), and convex games (supermodular: adding a player to a bigger coalition is more valuable).

nodes.label: The Core
nodes.note: The core is the set of allocations no coalition can block by deviating and doing better on their own. Core outcomes are always efficient (maximise social welfare), but the core may be empty.

nodes.label: Shapley Value
nodes.note: The Shapley value assigns each player their average marginal contribution over all orderings of players. It is the unique allocation satisfying efficiency, null player, symmetry, and additivity.

nodes.label: Banzhaf Index
nodes.note: The Banzhaf index averages marginal contributions over all coalitions (not orderings). It satisfies null player, symmetry, and additivity, but not efficiency. Used as a power index in simple games.

nodes.label:
Imputations &
Individual Rationality
nodes.note: An imputation is an outcome satisfying individual rationality: every player receives at least what they could get alone. It is the minimum acceptability condition for any allocation.

nodes.label:
Superadditive &
Convex Games
nodes.note: Superadditive games ensure the grand coalition is optimal (merging coalitions never loses value). Convex games further ensure that a player's marginal contribution increases as they join a larger coalition.

nodes.label:
Empty Core &
Stability Limits
nodes.note: Some games, like the three-player majority game, have empty cores. No allocation is immune to deviation by some coalition. In these cases, other stability concepts or fairness criteria must be used.

charts.title: Checking if an Allocation is in the Core
charts.description: Procedure for verifying whether a given payoff vector is in the core of a TU game.

nodes.label:
Start: TU game (N, v)
and payoff vector x
nodes.note: We have a TU game with characteristic function v and a proposed allocation x. We want to check if x is in the core: no coalition should prefer to deviate.

nodes.label:
Check efficiency:
sum of x equals v(N)?
nodes.note: The allocation must distribute exactly v(N), the value of the grand coalition. If the sum is less than v(N), the grand coalition can deviate and do better collectively.

nodes.label:
Check individual rationality:
x_i >= v({i}) for all i?
nodes.note: Every player must receive at least what they could achieve as a singleton coalition. If any player gets less than v({i}), they would prefer to go alone.

nodes.label:
Enumerate all
coalitions C in 2^N
nodes.note: For the core check, every subset of players must be verified. There are 2^n coalitions in total. For small n this is feasible; for large n it becomes exponentially hard.

nodes.label:
Take next
unverified coalition C
nodes.note: Consider the next coalition C that has not yet been checked for a blocking deviation.

nodes.label:
Does C block?
sum x_i(C) < v(C)?
nodes.note: Coalition C blocks if the total payoff its members receive under x is strictly less than v(C), meaning C can generate more value itself than it is allocated.

nodes.label:
More coalitions
to check?
nodes.note: If C did not block, check the remaining coalitions. If all have been verified, the allocation is in the core.

nodes.label:
Allocation IS
in the core
nodes.note: Every coalition receives at least its characteristic function value under x. The allocation is stable: no group of players has an incentive to deviate.

nodes.label:
Allocation NOT in core
Coalition C blocks
nodes.note: Coalition C can do better by itself than what it receives under x. The allocation is not in the core and is therefore unstable.

charts.title: Computing the Shapley Value
charts.description: Step-by-step procedure for computing each player's Shapley value in a TU game.

nodes.label:
Start: TU game (N, v)
n players
nodes.note: We want to compute the Shapley value for each player, which gives a unique fair allocation based on average marginal contributions.

nodes.label:
Select next
player i in N
nodes.note: Compute the Shapley value for player i. Repeat for all players.

nodes.label:
Enumerate all
n! permutations of N
nodes.note: List all n! orderings of the player set N. For each permutation, player i will join the coalition of all players who appear before i in that ordering.

nodes.label:
Take next
permutation pi
nodes.note: For this permutation pi, identify S(pi, i): the set of players appearing before player i in the ordering.

nodes.label:
Compute marginal
contribution: v(S+i) - v(S)
nodes.note: The marginal contribution of player i in permutation pi is v(S union {i}) - v(S), where S is the set of i's predecessors. This captures how much value i adds to the coalition already formed.

nodes.label:
More permutations
to process?
nodes.note: Continue processing permutations until all n! orderings have been considered for player i.

nodes.label:
Average marginal
contributions: divide by n!
nodes.note: Sum all marginal contributions for player i and divide by n!. This expected marginal contribution is the Shapley value phi_i.

nodes.label:
More players
to process?
nodes.note: Repeat the procedure for all remaining players until the complete Shapley value vector has been computed.

nodes.label:
Shapley value
vector complete
nodes.note: The Shapley value vector (phi_1, ..., phi_n) satisfies efficiency (sum = v(N)), null player, symmetry, and additivity. It is the unique fair allocation satisfying all four axioms.

charts.title: TU Game Classification: Superadditive and Convex
charts.description: How to classify a TU game as simple, superadditive, or convex using formal checks.

nodes.label:
Start: TU game
(N, v)
nodes.note: We want to classify the game according to structural properties that determine whether the grand coalition is incentivised and whether marginal contributions are increasing.

nodes.label:
Is v(C) in {0,1}
for all coalitions C?
nodes.note: A simple game has binary outcomes: each coalition is either winning (value 1) or losing (value 0). Weighted voting games are the canonical example.

nodes.label:
Check superadditivity:
v(S union T) >= v(S) + v(T)
for all disjoint S, T?
nodes.note: Superadditivity means merging coalitions never destroys value. This incentivises players to form the grand coalition, so we can assume the grand coalition forms.

nodes.label:
Check convexity:
v(A union B) + v(A intersect B)
>= v(A) + v(B) for all A, B?
nodes.note: Convexity (supermodularity) means a player's marginal contribution is non-decreasing as they join a larger coalition. Convexity implies superadditivity but not vice versa.

nodes.label:
SIMPLE game:
binary values, monotone
nodes.note: The game has 0-1 payoffs. The Shapley value becomes the Shapley-Shubik power index measuring the probability a player is a swing voter.

nodes.label:
SUPERADDITIVE:
grand coalition optimal
nodes.note: Merging coalitions never hurts. Players are incentivised to form the grand coalition. Payoff analysis can focus on allocations of v(N) among all players.

nodes.label:
CONVEX:
increasing marginal returns
nodes.note: The game is convex: joining a larger coalition is always at least as valuable. The core is non-empty, and the Shapley value always lies in the core for convex games.

nodes.label:
GENERAL game:
no special structure
nodes.note: The game has no special structure. The core may be empty, and computing the Shapley value requires enumerating all n! permutations.
